moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with this flexible knight move, which is the hallmark of the King's Indian Attack system. Nf3 controls the central squares e5 and d4 while keeping our options open—we haven't committed to a pawn structure yet. This move prepares a kingside fianchetto (g3 followed by Bg2), giving us a solid, patient setup that's particularly strong against 1...d5 and 1...e5 alike. The beauty of this opening is that we'll dictate the game's character through our setup rather than forcing early tactics.

Black mirrors your strategy by developing the knight to its best square, controlling the important e4 and d5 squares while preparing their own kingside setup. This move is solid and flexible—it doesn't commit Black to a specific pawn structure, mirroring your own non-committal approach. You should expect Black to continue with ...g6, creating a symmetrical fianchetto structure where both sides will aim for control and piece coordination.

Now we commit to the fianchetto formation, preparing to place our bishop on g2 where it will exert long pressure down the long diagonal toward d5 and beyond. The move also prevents Black from playing ...h6 and ...Bg7 while we're still deciding our setup. By playing g3, we're signaling that we'll build a solid position with Bg2, possibly followed by d3, keeping the center closed and flexible while our fianchettoed bishop becomes our main piece of activity.

Black completes the symmetrical setup, committing to a kingside fianchetto that mirrors your own setup. With this move, Black prepares ...Bg7, which will place their own powerful bishop on the long diagonal to pressure d4 and the center. Both sides now have identical pawn structures on the kingside, and the game will typically revolve around who achieves better central control and piece coordination—expect a strategic, positional struggle where small advantages in piece placement and timing will matter greatly.
